sparse_text: So, let's see what a bay plan, cargo plan, or stowage plan is. More often, during cargo operations it will be called a discharging and loading plan, and how to determine the correct stowage position of containers on it. When comparing the plan with the ship, you should look forward towards the bow. The bay plan is a numbering system that gives a cross-sectional view of the arrangement of containers on a vessel, above and below the deck.

payload.doc_title: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial)
payload.citation: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 1: Bay Plan Definition (Discharging/Loading Plan) (slide p.1-3)

document: So, let's see what a bay plan, cargo plan, or stowage plan is. More often, during cargo operations it will be called a discharging and loading plan, and how to determine the correct stowage position of containers on it. When comparing the plan with the ship, you should look forward towards the bow. The bay plan is a numbering system that gives a cross-sectional view of the arrangement of containers on a vessel, above and below the deck.

sparse_text: A stowage position consists of 6 digits. The first two digits indicate the bay, the middle two indicate the row, and the last two represent the tier.

payload.doc_title: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial)
payload.citation: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 2: Six-Digit Stow Position Format (Bay-Row-Tier) (slide p.4-4)

document: A stowage position consists of 6 digits. The first two digits indicate the bay, the middle two indicate the row, and the last two represent the tier.

sparse_text: The bay starts from bay 01 and can continue, for example, up to bay 54 towards the stern, depending on the size of the vessel.

payload.doc_title: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial)
payload.citation: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 3: Bay Numbering & Range (slide p.5-5)

document: The bay starts from bay 01 and can continue, for example, up to bay 54 towards the stern, depending on the size of the vessel.

sparse_text: A 20-foot container will occupy odd-numbered bays, whereas 40-foot containers are numbered with even-numbered bays. Bay numbers are indicated by odd numbers from fore to aft; odd-numbered bays show 20-foot container bays. When a pair of 20-footer bays is used as a 40-footer bay (either fixed or convertible), this 40-footer bay is represented by an even number that lies between the odd numbers representing the 20-footer bays fore and aft.

payload.doc_title: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial)
payload.citation: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 4: 20' Odd Bays vs 40' Even Bays (slide p.6-15)

document: A 20-foot container will occupy odd-numbered bays, whereas 40-foot containers are numbered with even-numbered bays. Bay numbers are indicated by odd numbers from fore to aft; odd-numbered bays show 20-foot container bays. When a pair of 20-footer bays is used as a 40-footer bay (either fixed or convertible), this 40-footer bay is represented by an even number that lies between the odd numbers representing the 20-footer bays fore and aft.

sparse_text: The bay numbers can be found welded and painted on the ship's coamings or hatch covers. The row shows the position of the container across the width of the vessel. A container placed on the centerline is marked 00. Rows are odd-numbered on the starboard side like 01, 03, 05, 07; rows are even-numbered on the port side like 02, 04, 06, 08. Row numbers are counted from the centre line to portside or starboard. Tier numbers start from 02, which represents a standard-height container (8 feet or 8 feet 6 inches for 20-footers, 8 feet 6 inches for 40-footers) at the lowest location of a bay. For ordinary-height containers only even numbers are used. A row indicates the container's position across the ship's breadth. Row 00 (or 01) is at the ship's centre; even numbers increase outwards (02, 04, 06) on one side and odd numbers on the other. This system prevents confusion between port and starboard containers; cadets must read positions quickly during cargo operations.

payload.doc_title: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial)
payload.topic_title: Row Numbering (Centerline 00, Starboard Odd / Port Even)
payload.citation: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 5: Row Numbering (Centerline 00, Starboard Odd / Port Even) (slide p.8-22)

document: The bay numbers can be found welded and painted on the ship's coamings or hatch covers. The row shows the position of the container across the width of the vessel. A container placed on the centerline is marked 00. Rows are odd-numbered on the starboard side like 01, 03, 05, 07; rows are even-numbered on the port side like 02, 04, 06, 08. Row numbers are counted from the centre line to portside or starboard. Tier numbers start from 02, which represents a standard-height container (8 feet or 8 feet 6 inches for 20-footers, 8 feet 6 inches for 40-footers) at the lowest location of a bay. For ordinary-height containers only even numbers are used. A row indicates the container's position across the ship's breadth. Row 00 (or 01) is at the ship's centre; even numbers increase outwards (02, 04, 06) on one side and odd numbers on the other. This system prevents confusion between port and starboard containers; cadets must read positions quickly during cargo operations.

sparse_text: If the container is placed on deck, tier numbering normally starts from 82, 84, 86 and upward. Containers stowed under the deck are numbered 02, 04, 06 and so on, starting from the bottom. Row numbers are counted from the centre line to portside or starboard. Tier numbers start from 02, which represents a standard-height container (8 feet or 8 feet 6 inches for 20-footers, 8 feet 6 inches for 40-footers) at the lowest location of a bay. For ordinary-height containers only even numbers are used. A tier specifies the vertical level of the container within its stack. The first deck tier often starts at 82 or 84. Even numbers ensure clear vertical identification. Cadets must know hold vs deck tiers and ship-specific numbering. Putting it all together: decode a stow position such as Bay 14 (fore-aft), Tier 84 (on deck, lowest tier) or Tier 02 (bottom-most tier inside the hold).

payload.doc_title: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial)
payload.citation: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 6: Tier Numbering (Deck 82+, Hold 02+ from Bottom) (slide p.12-23)

document: If the container is placed on deck, tier numbering normally starts from 82, 84, 86 and upward. Containers stowed under the deck are numbered 02, 04, 06 and so on, starting from the bottom. Row numbers are counted from the centre line to portside or starboard. Tier numbers start from 02, which represents a standard-height container (8 feet or 8 feet 6 inches for 20-footers, 8 feet 6 inches for 40-footers) at the lowest location of a bay. For ordinary-height containers only even numbers are used. A tier specifies the vertical level of the container within its stack. The first deck tier often starts at 82 or 84. Even numbers ensure clear vertical identification. Cadets must know hold vs deck tiers and ship-specific numbering. Putting it all together: decode a stow position such as Bay 14 (fore-aft), Tier 84 (on deck, lowest tier) or Tier 02 (bottom-most tier inside the hold).

sparse_text: The bay numbers can be found welded and painted on the ship's coamings or hatch covers. The row shows the position of the container across the width of the vessel. A container placed on the centerline is marked 00. Rows are odd-numbered on the starboard side like 01, 03, 05, 07; rows are even-numbered on the port side like 02, 04, 06, 08. The row numbers can also be found welded and painted on the ship's hatch covers and in the holds.

document: The bay numbers can be found welded and painted on the ship's coamings or hatch covers. The row shows the position of the container across the width of the vessel. A container placed on the centerline is marked 00. Rows are odd-numbered on the starboard side like 01, 03, 05, 07; rows are even-numbered on the port side like 02, 04, 06, 08. The row numbers can also be found welded and painted on the ship's hatch covers and in the holds.

sparse_text: How to decipher a container stowage plan: cell positions are identified by three factors. Bay is counted in the direction from fore to aft. Row is counted in the direction from the ship's centre line to portside or starboard side. Tier is counted in the vertical direction from bottom upward.

payload.doc_title: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial)
payload.citation: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 8: Three-Factor Cell Identification (Fore-Aft / Centerline / Vertical) (slide p.14-14)

document: How to decipher a container stowage plan: cell positions are identified by three factors. Bay is counted in the direction from fore to aft. Row is counted in the direction from the ship's centre line to portside or starboard side. Tier is counted in the vertical direction from bottom upward.

sparse_text: Bay plan abbreviations include: Reefer, Over-size (out of gauge), Empty, Open Top, High-Cube, IMDG (dangerous goods), and Restow.

payload.doc_title: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial)

payload.citation: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 9: Bay Plan Abbreviations (Reefer, OOG, Empty, OT, HC, IMDG, Restow) (slide p.17-17)

document: Bay plan abbreviations include: Reefer, Over-size (out of gauge), Empty, Open Top, High-Cube, IMDG (dangerous goods), and Restow.

sparse_text: Let's do a little practice: figure out the stow position of a given container by reading its bay, row, and tier. Finding the location of cargo based on the bay plan: use the Bay-Row-Tier coordinates to locate any container on the cross-sectional plan. What is the Bay-Row-Tier system on a container vessel? Every container has a unique address: Bay - Row - Tier.

payload.doc_title: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial)
payload.topic_title: Finding Cargo Location from the Bay Plan
payload.citation: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 10: Finding Cargo Location from the Bay Plan (slide p.13-19)

document: Let's do a little practice: figure out the stow position of a given container by reading its bay, row, and tier. Finding the location of cargo based on the bay plan: use the Bay-Row-Tier coordinates to locate any container on the cross-sectional plan. What is the Bay-Row-Tier system on a container vessel? Every container has a unique address: Bay - Row - Tier.

sparse_text: Why does Bay-Row-Tier exist? It is essential for managing thousands of containers onboard: knowing exact container storage, understanding load and discharge plans, and coordinating with crane operators, officers, and stevedores. The Bay-Row-Tier system provides a unique coordinate for each slot.

payload.doc_title: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial)
payload.citation: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 11: Why Bay-Row-Tier Exists (Unique Coordinate) (slide p.20-20)

document: Why does Bay-Row-Tier exist? It is essential for managing thousands of containers onboard: knowing exact container storage, understanding load and discharge plans, and coordinating with crane operators, officers, and stevedores. The Bay-Row-Tier system provides a unique coordinate for each slot.

sparse_text: What is a bay (fore-aft position)? A bay is a section of the ship cut across its width. Bays are odd-numbered (01, 03, 05). A 40-foot container occupies two 20-foot bay spaces. Familiarize yourself with your ship's bay numbering and follow the bay plan during cargo watches.

payload.doc_title: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial)
payload.topic_title: Bay as Fore-Aft Section; 40' Occupies Two 20' Spaces
payload.citation: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 12: Bay as Fore-Aft Section; 40' Occupies Two 20' Spaces (slide p.21-21)

document: What is a bay (fore-aft position)? A bay is a section of the ship cut across its width. Bays are odd-numbered (01, 03, 05). A 40-foot container occupies two 20-foot bay spaces. Familiarize yourself with your ship's bay numbering and follow the bay plan during cargo watches.

sparse_text: A tier specifies the vertical level of the container within its stack. The first deck tier often starts at 82 or 84. Even numbers ensure clear vertical identification. Cadets must know hold vs deck tiers and ship-specific numbering. Putting it all together: decode a stow position such as Bay 14 (fore-aft), Tier 84 (on deck, lowest tier) or Tier 02 (bottom-most tier inside the hold).

payload.doc_title: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial)
payload.citation: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 13: Worked Decoding Example (Bay 14 / Tier 84 / Tier 02) (slide p.23-23)

document: A tier specifies the vertical level of the container within its stack. The first deck tier often starts at 82 or 84. Even numbers ensure clear vertical identification. Cadets must know hold vs deck tiers and ship-specific numbering. Putting it all together: decode a stow position such as Bay 14 (fore-aft), Tier 84 (on deck, lowest tier) or Tier 02 (bottom-most tier inside the hold).

sparse_text: Why deck cadets must understand this: it is crucial for accurate, safe, and efficient shipboard operations. Cargo watches - verify correct placement, container numbers, and prevent shifting. Lashing operations - identify correct stacks for securing containers. Reefer checks - locate reefers, read temperatures, and record data. Hatch operations - know which bays/holds are affected by cover removal. Mastering Bay-Row-Tier ensures speed and accuracy in all tasks.

payload.doc_title: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial)
payload.citation: Bay Plan & Bay-Row-Tier System (Container Ship Tutorial), Topic 14: Operational Importance for Deck Cadets (slide p.24-24)

document: Why deck cadets must understand this: it is crucial for accurate, safe, and efficient shipboard operations. Cargo watches - verify correct placement, container numbers, and prevent shifting. Lashing operations - identify correct stacks for securing containers. Reefer checks - locate reefers, read temperatures, and record data. Hatch operations - know which bays/holds are affected by cover removal. Mastering Bay-Row-Tier ensures speed and accuracy in all tasks.

sparse_text: How officers use Bay-Row-Tier: the Chief Officer relies on this system for critical decisions - cross-referencing container data, designating special container areas, and identifying and managing hazardous material locations. Learn this system early to avoid confusion, ensure correct guidance, and prevent delays, re-shifting, penalties, and safety risks.
